content:
# Ephesians Chapter 6 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

### v.1 — "obey your parents in the Lord" vs. "obey your parents because you belong to the Lord"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Children, obey your parents in [the] Lord, for this is right" | en kyriō — locative |
| LOGOS | "Children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Children, obey your parents because you belong to the Lord, for this is the right thing to do" | "Because you belong to the Lord" — causal interpretation; adds "the right thing to do" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. en kyriō is a locative phrase — "in the Lord" — describing the sphere within which obedience occurs. PNEUMA interprets causally ("because you belong to the Lord") and expands "right" to "the right thing to do."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA interprets locative as causal.

---

### v.5 — "slaves" vs. "bondservants" vs. "slaves" — douloi

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Slaves, obey the lords according to [the] flesh" | douloi — "slaves" |
| LOGOS | "Bondservants, obey your earthly masters" | "Bondservants" — preserves kyrios wordplay |
| PNEUMA | "Slaves, obey your earthly masters with deep respect and fear" | "Deep respect and fear" — expands phobos kai tromos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Bondservants" captures both the literal slavery and the metaphorical service to Christ (v.6). PNEUMA expands "fear and trembling" (phobos kai tromos) to "deep respect and fear" — adding "deep" and replacing "trembling" with a weaker term.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — word choice for douloi.

---

### v.6 — "not with eye-service" vs. "Try to please them all the time"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not with eye-service as people-pleasers, but as slaves of Christos" | mē kat' ophthalmodoulian hōs anthrōpareskoi |
| LOGOS | "not by the way of eye-service, as people-pleasers" | Same — preserves compound terms |
| PNEUMA | "Try to please them all the time, not just when they are watching you" | Restructures; "all the time" — adds |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul's compound words (ophthalmodouleia = "eye-slavery," anthrōpareskoi = "people-pleasers") are vivid coinages. PNEUMA restructures into "try to please them all the time, not just when they are watching you" — reversing Paul's negative framing and adding "all the time."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA reverses negative framing.

---

### v.12 — "world-rulers of this darkness" vs. "cosmic powers over this present darkness" vs. "mighty powers in this dark world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the world-rulers of this darkness" | kosmokratoras tou skotous toutou |
| LOGOS | "the cosmic powers over this present darkness" | "Cosmic powers" — captures kosmokratōr |
| PNEUMA | "evil rulers and authorities of the unseen world, against mighty powers in this dark world, and against evil spirits in the heavenly places" | Expands; adds "unseen," "evil" (×2), "mighty" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul lists four categories of spiritual adversaries in an escalating sequence. PNEUMA adds multiple adjectives not in the Greek: "evil" (twice), "unseen," "mighty." Paul's list is stark — the enemies are named without descriptive embellishment.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds multiple adjectives.

---

### v.15 — "the readiness of the gospel of peace" vs. "the peace that comes from the Good News"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having shod your feet with the readiness of the gospel of peace" | tē hetoimasia tou euangeliou tēs eirēnēs |
| LOGOS | "having put on the readiness given by the gospel of peace" | "Readiness given by" — genitive of source |
| PNEUMA | "put on the peace that comes from the Good News so that you will be fully prepared" | "Peace" becomes the object; "fully prepared" — adds |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek has "the readiness (hetoimasia) of the gospel of peace" — what is put on is readiness, not peace. PNEUMA makes "peace" the direct object and adds "fully prepared" — shifting the structure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA shifts the object from readiness to peace.

---

### v.19 — "the mystery of the gospel" vs. "God's mysterious plan that the Good News is for Jews and Gentiles alike"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in boldness to make known the mystery of the gospel" | to mystērion tou euangeliou |
| LOGOS | "boldly to proclaim the mystery of the gospel" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "boldly explain God's mysterious plan that the Good News is for Jews and Gentiles alike" | Specifies the mystery content; adds "for Jews and Gentiles alike" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Paul asks for boldness to proclaim "the mystery of the gospel." PNEUMA specifies the mystery's content ("that the Good News is for Jews and Gentiles alike") — a correct interpretation (cf. 3:6), but Paul leaves it compressed here. The addition turns a prayer request into an explanatory statement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA specifies what Paul leaves compressed.

---

### v.23 — "peace to the brothers" vs. "peace be with you, dear brothers and sisters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Peace to the brothers and love with faith" | tois adelphois — "to the brothers" |
| LOGOS | "Peace be to the brothers, and love with faith" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Peace be with you, dear brothers and sisters, and may God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ give you love with faithfulness" | Adds "dear," "and sisters"; "faithfulness" for pisteōs |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "dear" and "and sisters" (not in Greek — adelphois here), and changes "faith" (pistis) to "faithfulness" — a different concept. Paul's benediction is formal and compact.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds terms and changes pistis.

---

### v.24 — "in incorruptibility" vs. "with love incorruptible" vs. "eternally"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "all the [ones] loving our Lord Yeshua Christos in incorruptibility" | en aphtharsia — "in incorruptibility" |
| LOGOS | "all who love our Lord Jesus Christ with love incorruptible" | "Love incorruptible" — renders as qualifier of love |
| PNEUMA | "May God's grace be eternally with all who love our Lord Jesus Christ" | "Eternally" — replaces incorruptibility with time concept |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. en aphtharsia ("in incorruptibility") is the letter's final phrase. LOGOS takes it as qualifying the love — "with love incorruptible." PNEUMA replaces with "eternally" — importing a temporal concept where Paul uses an ontological one. Incorruptibility (aphtharsia) is about the nature of the love, not its duration.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. aphtharsia (incorruptibility) is an ontological category — the love is imperishable in nature. PNEUMA's "eternally" shifts to a temporal category. Paul's closing word is about the quality of love, not just its length.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 4 | "Because you belong to" (v.1), "unseen/evil/mighty" (v.12), "Jews and Gentiles alike" (v.19), "dear/sisters" (v.23) |
| PNEUMA structural changes | 2 | Reverses negative framing (v.6), shifts object from readiness to peace (v.15) |
| PNEUMA word replacements | 2 | "Faithfulness" for "faith" (v.23), "eternally" for "in incorruptibility" (v.24) |
| Ontological vs. temporal | 1 | aphtharsia: incorruptibility vs. eternity (v.24) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 6's most significant disagreement is the closing word — aphtharsia (v.24). Paul ends the letter on "incorruptibility," an ontological term about the nature of love. PNEUMA converts this to "eternally," shifting from what the love is to how long it lasts. The armor passage (v.12) also shows PNEUMA's tendency to add descriptive adjectives where Paul's language is deliberately stark. Paul names the cosmic adversaries without embellishment — the bare naming is itself powerful.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
